title: Quantification of Total Phosphorus Content in Plant Material Using Microwave Digestion and Segmented Flow Analysis
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"3euwz","text":"This protocol aims to quantify the total phosphorus (P) concentration in leaf material through acid digestion and segmented flow analysis. This method has been tested on plant species including Australian Eucalyptus and other native understory species but could be applicable to foliar leaf material in general with minor modifications. Phosphorus content is often measured using a Kjeldahl digestion, which can be time consuming; we present this microwave digestion method using nitric acid and hydrogen peroxide as an alternative to reduce sample digestion times. This method is limited by the number of positions available in the microwave digestion rotor. Phases of analysis include: (1) acid digestion using microwave digestor (3 hours); (2) preparation of reagents (2 hours); and, (3) analysis for total P on the flow analyser using antimony-phospho-molybdate complexation reaction (30 minutes plus 90 seconds per sample).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":205,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"qrbnn","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"wiglw","text":"Phosphorus (P) is an essential nutrient for plant growth and is involved in processes ranging from photosynthesis to root development (Vance et al. 2003).  Phosphorus is frequently used to make commercial flame retardants, and research suggests that it may have a similar role in trees and leaves, that is, to reduce the flammability of leaves (Scarff \u0026 Westoby 2008, Scarff et al. 2012). This protocol uses microwave digestion as a more rapid digestion alternative to the traditional Kjeldahl digestion, and allows the processing of approximately 200 samples per week.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5ij1h","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7npgz","text":"Microwave digestion has several advantages over open-vessel Kjeldahl digestion, in addition to its increased digestion speed. The closed vessels allow the digestions to be run overnight without supervision. Additionally, the Teflon vessels can be readily cleaned and dried, resulting in a short turnaround time. The efficacy of microwave digestion for determining total P in plant material using a nitric acid hydrogen peroxide digest was tested using the NIST standard reference material 1575, Pine Needles, detecting approximately 100% of the stated P concentration.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fc5on","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ylgvf","text":"Digested samples should be analysed for P without delay. The range for analysis is 0.1-10 mg P/L (Skalar, 2023). Sufficient personal protective equipment, such as face shields, acid gloves, and scrubbed fume hoods are all required for this procedure. Other useful protocols for reference include Younes \u0026 Cain (2024) which outlines the process of drying and grinding plant material for analysis. Additionally, a protocol outlining a traditional Kjeldahl digestion for phosphorus extraction is presented in Cain \u0026 Younes (2024) if microwave digestion is not available. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"tvj10","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: To a microwave digestion tube, add 0.1 g ( + 0.01 g) of ground, homogenised plant material. See Figure 1 for example of digestion vessels.
Add 9 mL of concentrated nitric acid and 1 mL of hydrogen peroxide to the plant material.

Allow the mixture to react in the vessel open in the fume hood for ~45 minutes. NOTE: Samples will effervesce and release gas. Some samples will effervesce strongly and may require that half the acid and hydrogen peroxide are added and allowed to react for 30 minutes before the other half of the acid and peroxide are added. This is to prevent samples spilling over the top of the digestion vessel.
Seal the microwave digestion vessels and digest for 15 minutes at 200°C.

Open vessels in the (ideally, scrubbed) fume hood once they have cooled below 60°C. Warning: Loosen the digestion tubes from their frames (if applicable) slowly as acid bubbles and aerosols may emerge from the seal between vessel and lid as pressure in the vessel is released. Once bubbling has stopped open the vessels. Toxic yellow/orange/brown fumes (NOx gases) will be released when lid is removed so vessels should be opened in a scrubbed fume cupboard. Approximately 9mL of solution will remain.
Fill the 50 mL conical tubes with 36 mL of MilliQ water so that acid digests are diluted to a lower concentration of P for analysis.
Pour the acid mixture of each digestion tube into a 50 mL conical tube. Store at 4°C until ready for analysis.
All reagents can be scaled down depending on how many samples need to be ran. Reagents and standards are prepared as outlined in Fishman & Friedman (1989), O’Dell (1993) and Skalar (2023).
Diluent: In a 5 L vessel add ~4.9 L of MilliQ water. Add 90 mL of concentrated nitric acid and 10 mL of hydrogen peroxide to mimic the background acid concentration of samples. Stability 1 month.
Sulphuric acid solution: Fill a 1 L volumetric flask with 800 mL of MilliQ water. Add 40 mL of sulphuric acid and dilute to the 1 L mark. Add 2 mL of FFD6. Stability 1 week.
Distilled water and FFD6: Fill a 1 L volumetric flask with MilliQ water then add 2 mL of FFD6 and mix. Stability 1 week.

Ammonium heptamolybdate solution: Add 800 mL Milli-Q water to a 1L volumetric flask and add 230 mg of potassium antimony oxide tartrate and swirl to dissolve. Add 69 mL of sulphuric acid (98%) and leave to cool. Add 6g of ammonium heptamolybdate using a plastic spoon. Swirl to mix. Dilute to the 1 L mark with Milli-Q water then add 2 mL of FFD6. Stability 1 week.
Ascorbic Acid Solution: Add 800 mL Milli-Q water to a 1 L volumetric flask, then add 11 g Ascorbic acid. Swirl to dissolve. Dilute to 1 L using Milli-Q water. Stability 1 week, keep refrigerated.
Preparation of standards must be done on the day samples are being run. Make new standards each day.

Phosphorus Stock Solution (1000 mg P/L): Weight out 4.394 g of potassium dihydrogen phosphate. Add 800 mL Milli-Q water to a 1 L volumetric flask, then add the potassium dihydrogen phosphate. Dilute to the 1 L mark with Milli-Q water. Stable for 1 month.
Phosphorus Working Solution (10 mg P/L): Dilute 0.3 mL of stock solution to 30 mL using MilliQ water.

Using the autosampler (Skalar SA1074 Autosampler Random Access) program the preparation of standards 1-7 to be made up from the working solution. Figure 2 outlines the concentrations of standards that should be run for analysis.
First run all lines with Milli-Q water to check for leaks.
Run reagents through the reaction manifold for approximately 10 minutes to prep the lines.

Add working standard and samples into the autosampler and run, as per the manufacturer’s instructions, using the 880 nm detector to detect for orthophosphate. Samples should be diluted 4-fold from the diluted digest samples.
At the end of a run, run Milli-Q water through all lines for approximately 10 mins. Turn off machine.
Assess peaks – if peaks are outside the calibration curve range they need to be further diluted and re-run.

Calculate phosphorus content as a percentage of plant material using the following equation: Phosphorus (% of plant material) = (mg P/L * volume of acid digest (L) * dilution factor) / weight of plant material (mg) * 100
See Figure 3 for troubleshooting advice for this procedure.

Nitric acid digestion – total time three hours Weigh out samples into digestion vessels and add acid and peroxide, takes approximately one hour for 15 samples Allow plant material and acid to react in an open vessel for 45 minutes Digest samples and allow to cool, approximately one hour. Pour samples into water, takes 15 minutes Analyse digested samples using flow analysis chemistry – one day Prepare reagents, takes approximately two hours Place samples into flow analysis manifold sample tubes and enter into table, takes approximately four to five hours to run ~120 samples

Analysis of NIST standard reference material 1575a, Pine needles, using a nitric acid microwave digestion and the Skalar SAN segmented flow analyser yielded a total P content of 0.14 mg P. Expected results were 0.12 + 0.02 mg P (NIST, 1993). This indicates a complete digestion of ground foliar material using nitric acid microwave digestion and accurate measurement of P using colorimetry. A 20-fold dilution of pure plant material/acid digest solution typically ensures sample peaks fall within the calibration curve during analysis. Occasionally some peaks will be above the calibration curve range and should be further diluted for analysis. For eucalyptus leaf samples values between 0-0.41 mg P were obtained.

Figure 1. Example of teflon vessel used for microwave digestion of plant material. Figure 2. Concentration of calibration standards for analysis of total phosphorus in plant material using segmented flow analysis. Figure 3. Common issues with and suggested solutions for microwave digestion and analysis of total P using flow analysis chemistry.
